title: Little Library / Community Pantry Box
summary: A weatherproof post-mounted box with a windowed door — half a sheet of exterior plywood, built to survive outdoors with books or canned goods inside.
description:
A little free library is a simple box asked to do a hard job: sit in the weather for years, open several times a day, and keep paper dry. Everything unusual in this plan exists for that job. The roof slants and overhangs (the cut list even upsizes it from the drawings for more coverage). The door gets an acrylic window so people can see what's inside without opening it into the rain. Weatherstripping seals the door's top and sides — but deliberately NOT the bottom, because a box that can't drain the water that does get in is a terrarium.

Use actual exterior plywood (CDX or AC) and exterior glue, fill every edge void, and topcoat with a spar urethane — the flexible marine-style finish that tolerates the plywood's outdoor movement where furniture poly would crack. Paint bright; the box is signage for itself.

The same box, mounted lower, is a community pantry — the shelf height is the only design decision that changes (cans are shorter than cookbooks). For mounting, the plan suggests a 5-foot 4x4 buried 2 feet, or a 3-foot post set in a heavy planter if you can't dig. Either way, the box screws down through its bottom into the post top — gravity is not a fastener in a public installation.
timeLabel: 6–9 hrs including finishing — the paint and urethane take longer than the build

tools.note: Breaking the plywood into strips, then crosscutting the parts. A straightedge guide keeps the rips honest.
The 1x3 door frame is pocket-holed at the corners — the cleanest joint this small frame can get.
The slanted roof profile on the sides — a circular saw finishes short of the corner; the jigsaw cleans it up.
Drill the acrylic's screw holes slightly oversize so wood movement doesn't crack it.
Exterior ply is rough — sand well before paint, and ease every edge the public will touch.

materials.name: Exterior plywood, 3/4", 48" x 48" (CDX or AC)
materials.note: Half a sheet does the whole carcass and roof.

materials.name: 1x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Front trim and top strips — 12 board feet total.

materials.name: 4x4 post, 6 ft
materials.note: 5 ft buried 2 ft in the ground; or a 3-footer set in a heavy planter with 2 ft of topsoil.

materials.name: Acrylic glass, 15" x 16-1/4"
materials.note: The door window. Acrylic, not glass — this is a public box.

materials.name: Exterior clasp
materials.note: Holds the door snug against the weatherstripping.

materials.name: Weatherstripping tape
materials.note: Top and sides of the door opening — never the bottom; the bottom is the drain.

materials.name: Exterior wood filler
materials.note: Fill the plywood edge voids or they'll wick water under the paint.

materials.name: Spar urethane
materials.note: The flexible exterior clear coat over everything — furniture poly cracks outdoors.

cutList.note: Cut the slant roof profile down to 18-7/8" at the low edge — see step 1.
Drawn at 17-1/2" but cut bigger — more overhang, more weather coverage.

steps.title: Break down the plywood
steps.body:
Rip the half-sheet into two strips — one 17" wide, one 20" wide — then crosscut the parts and LABEL each one: bottom, shelf, back, two sides, roof.

For the sides, mark the low edge at 18-7/8" and cut the triangle off the top to create the slanted roof profile. The two sides must match — cut one, and use it to mark the other.

steps.title: Join the bottom and back
steps.body: Screw and glue the bottom to the back with the 2" screws. Exterior glue on every joint in this build — the fasteners hold it together, the glue keeps water out of the joints.

steps.title: Add the sides
steps.body: Attach the sides to the bottom and back with 2" screws, slanted profile running down toward the front. The box takes shape here — check it sits flat and square before the glue sets.

steps.title: Add the top strips
steps.body: Screw the 1x2 strips across the top of the opening front and back. They close the gap between the sides at the roofline and give the roof panel something to fasten to along its edges.

steps.title: Attach the roof
steps.body: Glue and screw the roof panel down through into the sides and top strips with the 1-1/4" pocket screws (used here as short wood screws). The overhang goes to the front and sides — it's the box's umbrella; make it even.

steps.title: Set the shelf
steps.body: Install the interior shelf at whatever height suits the mission: taller bays for cookbooks and picture books, shorter for cans and boxes. It doesn't have to be centered — an asymmetric split is usually more useful.

steps.title: Trim the front
steps.body: Frame the front opening with 1x2s — best built as a pocket-holed frame first, then attached to the box with glue and trim screws. This frame is what the door closes against and what the weatherstripping later seals to, so keep it flat and flush.

steps.title: Build and hang the door
steps.body:
Pocket-hole the 1x3 door frame together, then screw the acrylic panel to its back — drill the acrylic slightly oversize so the screws don't crack it when the wood moves.

Hang the door on the exterior hinges, add the handle and the clasp. The clasp isn't decoration: it's what pulls the door tight against the weatherstripping in a storm.

steps.title: Finish hard, seal smart, then mount
steps.body:
Fill every plywood edge void with exterior filler, sand, paint, and topcoat everything with two coats of spar urethane — the finish is what decides whether this box lasts three years or fifteen.

After finishing, run weatherstripping tape along the TOP and SIDES of the door opening only. Leave the bottom open on purpose: any water that gets in must be able to leave.

Mount on the 4x4 — 5 ft post buried 2 ft, or 3 ft post set in a planter under 2 ft of soil — and screw the box down through its bottom into the post. Stock it, and watch the neighborhood find it.

images.alt: Post-mounted little free library box with slanted roof and windowed door
